items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-2-1, visual separation may be applied when other approved separation is assured _____ and after the application of visual separation.
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, other approved separation must be assured both before AND after the application of visual separation. This is the foundational precondition: visual separation bridges a gap between periods of approved separation.

choices.text: before

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1, which factor is NOT among those controllers must consider to ensure other separation will exist?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, the listed factors are: aircraft performance, wake turbulence, closure rate, routes of flight, known weather conditions, and aircraft position. Aircraft weight category is not on this list (though wake turbulence is, and weight category relates to it).

choices.text: Aircraft position.
Closure rate.
Aircraft weight category.
Routes of flight.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1, up to what altitude may visual separation be applied in terminal airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a, terminal visual separation may be applied up to but not including FL180. This is the same altitude limit as the en route provision in 7-2-1b, but the procedures for applying it differ between terminal and en route.

choices.text: Up to and including FL180.
Up to but not including FL180.
Up to and including FL240.
There is no altitude limit; visual separation may be applied at any altitude where VMC exists.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a1(e), when is tower-applied visual separation not authorized?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a1(e), tower-applied visual separation is not authorized when wake turbulence separation is required. This is in addition to the blanket prohibition on visual separation when the lead aircraft is a super (from the body paragraph).

choices.text: When either aircraft is IFR at an airport without an operating control tower.
When wake turbulence separation is required.
When both aircraft are on converging courses.
When radar contact has been lost on either aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a1(d), when must tower-applied visual separation NOT be applied between successive departures?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a1(d), visual separation must not be applied between successive departures when departure routes and/or aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation. Even if the tower can see both aircraft, if their flight paths or performance differences would prevent maintaining visual separation, it cannot be used.

choices.text: When the departures are from different airports.
When the first departure is a heavy and the second is a small.
When departure routes and/or aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation.
When the tower does not have DBRITE radar available.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a2 pilot-applied visual separation, what must the controller do when aircraft are on converging courses?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a2(d), when aircraft are on converging courses, the controller must inform the other aircraft of the traffic and that visual separation is being applied. The phraseology includes 'HAS YOU IN SIGHT AND WILL MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.'

choices.text: Vector one aircraft away from the converging course to re-establish radar separation.
Instruct both pilots to report each other in sight before continuing.
Inform the other aircraft of the traffic and that visual separation is being applied.
Cancel visual separation and revert to IFR separation standards.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-2-1a2(e), advise the pilots if the targets appear likely to _____.
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a2(e), the controller must advise pilots when targets appear likely to merge. For terminal, the example phrase is 'Targets appear likely to merge.' For en route, it is 'Radar targets appear likely to merge.'

choices.text: merge

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1b EN ROUTE, how does the traffic advisory for converging course traffic differ from the terminal version?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1b phraseology, the en route converging-course advisory includes 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' in the transmission: '(ACID) TRAFFIC, (clock position and distance), (direction)-BOUND, (type of aircraft), ON CONVERGING COURSE, HAS YOU IN SIGHT AND WILL MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.' The terminal version (7-2-1a2) does not include 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' in the phraseology.

choices.text: The en route version includes 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' in the transmission; the terminal version does not include those words.
The en route version omits the aircraft type; the terminal version includes it.
The en route version requires the controller to issue a vector; the terminal version does not.
There is no difference between the en route and terminal converging-course phraseology.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1b EN ROUTE, what must the controller advise pilots about regarding aircraft type?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1b(g), the en route controller must advise the pilots if either aircraft is a heavy. This is in addition to issuing wake turbulence cautionary advisories per 2-1-20 (7-2-1b(h)). Terminal procedures also require traffic advisories but the heavy advisory is explicitly listed only in the en route section.

choices.text: Advise if either aircraft is a super.
Advise if either aircraft is a heavy.
Advise if either aircraft is a large.
Advise only the aircraft type of the traffic, without weight-category distinctions.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1c, what procedures must a nonapproach control tower apply when providing visual separation?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1c, nonapproach control towers must apply the procedures contained in subparagraph a1 (tower-applied) or a2 (pilot-applied) when providing visual separation. They have the option of either method.

choices.text: Only the tower-applied procedures in subparagraph a1.
Only the pilot-applied procedures in subparagraph a2.
The procedures in subparagraph a1 or a2, depending on the situation.
A simplified version of visual separation that does not reference a1 or a2.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1d, what must a nonapproach tower controller state to the radar controller to obtain approval for visual separation?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1d, the nonapproach tower controller must state that they will provide visual separation between arrivals, departures/arrivals, and/or successive departures, AND state the call signs of all aircraft involved. The radar controller can then approve.

choices.text: State only the flight rules of the aircraft involved.
State the call signs of all aircraft involved and the type of visual separation being applied.
State that they will provide visual separation and state the call signs of all aircraft involved.
State only that visual separation is requested, without specifying call signs.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1, who is responsible for IFR separation before and after the application of visual separation when a nonapproach control tower accepts visual separation authorization?
items.explanation: Per the note in 7-2-1, separation of IFR aircraft before and after application of visual separation is an IFR control function that must be applied by the Approach/Departure/En Route facility. The nonapproach tower becomes responsible for the visual separation itself, but the radar facility retains responsibility for IFR separation before and after.

choices.text: The nonapproach control tower, because accepting visual separation transfers all separation responsibility.
The pilot, because visual separation transfers separation responsibility to the flight crew.
The Approach/Departure/En Route facility.
Both the tower and radar facility share IFR separation responsibility jointly.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a1(a), what communication requirement must be met for tower-applied visual separation?

choices.text: Maintain communication with both aircraft at all times, with no exceptions.
Maintain communication with at least one of the aircraft involved, or ensure ability to communicate immediately with applicable military aircraft per 3-9-3a2.
Relay all instructions through the approach control frequency; the tower is not required to maintain direct communication.
Maintain communication only with the trailing aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a1(a), the tower must maintain communication with at least one aircraft or ensure immediate communication ability with applicable military aircraft per 3-9-3a2. Communication with both is not required.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a1(f), what is required for ATCTs at adjacent airports to apply visual separation between their traffic and the other facility's traffic?

choices.text: Authorization and adherence to all provisions of 7-2-1a1.
Only a verbal coordination between the tower supervisors.
A separate waiver from the Service Area office because adjacent-airport visual separation is generally prohibited.
Direct radar coverage from a single TRACON that controls both airports.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a1(f), ATCTs at adjacent airports may be authorized to apply visual separation between their traffic and the other facility's traffic, provided all provisions of 7-2-1a1 still apply.

items.situation: A terminal controller is providing tower-applied visual separation between two IFR arrivals. One aircraft reports the other in sight and asks to maintain visual separation. The controller knows that wake turbulence separation would normally be required between these two aircraft due to weight categories, but the conditions are VFR and both aircraft are in sight.
items.para_context: Per 7-2-1a1(e), tower-applied visual separation is not authorized when wake turbulence separation is required. Per 7-2-1a2, pilot-applied visual separation has different requirements.
items.question_text: Can the controller use visual separation in this situation?

choices.text: Yes, using pilot-applied visual separation under a2 since the pilot has the traffic in sight and will maintain separation.
Yes, using tower-applied visual separation under a1 since the tower can visually observe both aircraft.
No, because visual separation is never authorized when either aircraft is a heavy.
No, because visual separation is prohibited whenever wake turbulence is a factor, regardless of application method.

items.explanation: While tower-applied visual separation (a1) is not authorized when wake turbulence separation is required (a1(e)), pilot-applied visual separation (a2) does not have this restriction. The controller may use pilot-applied visual separation under a2 if the pilot meets the requirements.

items.instruction: The controller is using pilot-applied visual separation on converging courses in en route airspace. Which pilot readback is correct for the aircraft being informed about the converging traffic?
items.clearance: N456CD, TRAFFIC, TWO O'CLOCK, FIVE MILES, SOUTHBOUND, B737, ON CONVERGING COURSE, HAS YOU IN SIGHT AND WILL MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.

choices.text: N456CD, traffic in sight, we will maintain visual separation from the Boeing.
N456CD, roger, looking for the traffic.
N456CD, we have the traffic and accept the visual separation.
N456CD, traffic in sight at our two o'clock.

items.explanation: The correct readback acknowledges the traffic advisory. This aircraft is the one being informed about converging traffic and visual separation (not the one maintaining it). The pilot who is maintaining visual separation is the one who must state both 'in sight' and 'will maintain.' The other aircraft just receives the advisory.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1a2(f), what coordination is required to transfer control of an aircraft maintaining visual separation to an adjacent position, sector, or facility?

choices.text: A point-out on the radar display with acknowledgment.
Coordination procedures specified in an LOA or facility directive.
A verbal briefing between the transferring and receiving controllers on each transfer.
Control may not be transferred while visual separation is being used.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a2(f), control of aircraft maintaining visual separation may be transferred, but coordination procedures must be specified in an LOA or facility directive. This ensures standardized procedures rather than ad hoc transfers.

items.situation: An en route controller has a pilot maintaining visual separation from another aircraft. The radar targets begin to converge on the display. The controller has already issued the instruction to maintain visual separation but did not issue a merging advisory at that time.
items.para_context: Per 7-2-1b(f) and the associated note: 'Advise the pilots if the radar targets appear likely to merge.' The note states to issue this advisory in conjunction with the instruction to maintain visual separation, the advisory to the other aircraft of the converging course, or thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that targets are merging.
items.question_text: What should the controller do about the merging targets becoming apparent after the initial instruction?

choices.text: The controller must issue the merging advisory because the note allows issuing it 'thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that the targets are merging.'
The controller cannot issue a merging advisory now because it must be given only at the time of the initial visual separation instruction.
The controller should cancel visual separation and issue vectors, because merging targets indicate the procedure is unsafe.
No additional advisory is needed because the pilot already accepted visual separation responsibility.

items.explanation: Per the note to 7-2-1b(f), the merging advisory can be issued 'thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that the targets are merging.' This allows the controller to issue it later if the merging condition was not apparent initially.
